story.title: Dr. Yamamoto's Earthquake Shake Lab
story.text:
My name is Dr. Takeshi Yamamoto. I am an earthquake engineer at a big university lab. My team and I work to keep people safe during earthquakes. We ask a big question: Why do some buildings fall while others stand tall?

The answer is smart Engineering Design. A big Earthquake Magnitude creates a very strong Shaking Force. This force pushes and pulls on a building's frame. If the force is too strong, it causes major Structural Damage. Walls crack and floors can collapse. When a building has more damage, the people inside have less safety.

Our solution is to give buildings more Flexibility. A flexible building can bend and sway with the shaking. It moves with the earthquake's energy instead of breaking apart. More Flexibility leads to much less damage. One of my favorite tools is Base Isolation. We put special rubber and steel pads under the building. During a quake, the ground moves wildly, but the building slides gently on top. We test every idea on our giant shake tables. This helps us build safer cities for the future.
think_abouts: How does a flexible building protect people inside?
What would happen to a stiff, brick building in a strong earthquake?
story.go_deeper: Besides flexibility and base isolation, what other ideas could make a building safer from earthquakes?

margin_defs.definition: A person who designs and builds things to solve problems.
Special pads under a building that let it slide to absorb shaking.

check_prompts: What is one way engineers add flexibility to buildings to keep people safe?

vocabulary.definition: A number that measures how strong an earthquake is.
vocabulary.use_sentence: A high earthquake magnitude means the ground will shake very hard.

vocabulary.definition: The power to bend or sway without breaking.
vocabulary.use_sentence: The tall grass has a lot of flexibility, so it does not break in the wind.

vocabulary.definition: The push and pull from the ground that hits a building during an earthquake.
vocabulary.use_sentence: The strong shaking force made the windows rattle.

vocabulary.definition: When parts of a building crack, break, or fall down.
vocabulary.use_sentence: After the earthquake, we saw structural damage like cracks in the walls.

vocabulary.definition: A plan that engineers make to solve a problem using science and math.
vocabulary.use_sentence: The engineering design for the new bridge helps it stay safe in strong winds.

discussion_cards.prompt: If you were an engineer like Dr. Yamamoto, what would you build to test your earthquake-safe building idea?
discussion_cards.frame: To test my idea, I would build a model of a ___ and put it on a ___.

discussion_cards.prompt: Why is it more important for a building to be flexible than to be very stiff during an earthquake?
discussion_cards.frame: It is more important for a building to be flexible because it can ___ instead of ___.

discussion_cards.prompt: Dr. Yamamoto mentioned 'Base Isolation.' How is this like putting a building on roller skates?
discussion_cards.frame: Base isolation is like roller skates because the building can ___ while the ground ___.

reflection_frames.knew: Before reading, I already knew that ___
reflection_frames.learned: From the story, I learned that ___
